content.full_text:
Fern Porrrrcel PnecrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-56ffi . Fax (916) 322-0886
April 16,2004
Brad Castillo
1599 East Roberts
Fresno, CA93710
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-04-069
FACTS
You are a local city council member for the City of Fresno, Council District 4.
You have been contacted by a company that does laser correction (LASIK) eye surgery.
They, in conjunction with a local Spanish television station, want you to participatein
having the eye surgery done, if you are a good candidate. They want a Sp^anish ipeaking
individual who wears glasses and have asked you to participate. The laser company plans
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
Dear Mr. Castillo:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the gift
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the..Act,,).1
QUESTION
Would the provision of services related to LASIK eye strgery, and the surgery
itself, at no cost to you, for purposes of filming the process and procedure for fufure use
by the laser company and,lor a local Spanish television station, constitute a gft, income or
a prohibited honorarium?
CONCLUSION
Because you would be receiving a personal benefit which is not available to
members of the public without regard to official status, the surgery and related services
would constitute a gift to you, unless you can prove that you provided consideration of
equal or greater value. If you will make an oral presentation as part of the documentary,
you may not receive the payment unless it constitutes earned income.


FileNo. A-04-069
Page No. 2
on documenting this on film as you go through the pre-examination, surgery and post-
examination and no cost will be incurred by you.
ANALYSIS
Gift or Income
Section 89503(a) provides:
'T.{o elected state officer, elected officer of a local
government agency, or other individual specified in Section
87200 shall accept gifts from any single source in any
calendar year with a total value of more than two hundred
fifty dollars ($250)."
As a member of a local city council, you are an elected officer of a local
goveflrment agency. Thus, the prohibition on accepting gifts over the statutorily
determined value from a single source is applicable to you. The current gift limit is $340
per year.
Forpurposes of this letter, we have assumed that the cost of the LASIK eye
surgery being offered to you is in excess of $340.
Section 82028 states, in pertinent part:
"(a) 'Gift'means . . . anypayment that confers a
personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that
consideration ofequal or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of
value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular
course of business to members of the public without regard
to official status."
Under section 82028, anything ofvalue grven to a public official without cost,
including the professional services of an eye surgeon, is a gift unless it is expressly
exempted from the definition of "gift". (See, e.g., BakerAdvice Letter, wo. ,q.-01-lg7;
Kolkey Advice LetteE No. I-95-13 4; and connor Advice Letter, No. A-94-247,
conceming legal services; Gould Advice Letter, No. I-95-320 (copies enclosed).)
Exernptions to the definition of "gift" are set forth in regulation tbgqZ (copy enclosed).
Your situation does not fall into any of the categories of exceptions listed.
Presumably, you were asked to be the subject of the LASIK documentary because
you are well known within the local Spanish-speaking community due to your official
status as a city council member, not simply because you are a Spanish-speaking
individual who wears glasses. Therefore, because this offer is not available in the regular


File No. A-04-069
Page No. 3
course ofbusiness to members of the public without regard to official status, the rebate or
discount exception does not apply.
Thus, the LASIK surgery would constitute a gift under the Act, unless you could
prove that you provided consideration of equal or greater value. (Section 82028.) If you
were able to prove that you provided the laser company and/or the television station, if
the television station is bearing any of the costs of the surgery or pre- or post-operative
care, then the cost of the surgery and any related care would constitute reportable income.
(Section 82030, copy enclosed.)
Ifonorarium
Under your facts, the laser company plans on documenting the surgery on film,
presumably for showing to a broader audience at some future date. If you wiit be making
an oral presentation as part of the documentary, you may not receive the payment at a[.
Section 89502 prohibits any elected officer of a local govemment ugerr"y from accepting
anyhonorarium. "Honorarium" is defined in section 89501(a) as follows:
*(a) For purposes of this chapter, .honorarium, means,
except as provided in subdirrision (b), any payment made in
consideration for any speech gvffi, article published, or
attendance at any public or private conference, convention,
meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering.,'
Regulation 18931.1 (copy enclosed) defines "speech given," in pertinent part, as
"a public address, oration, or other form of oral presentation, and includes participation in
apanel, seminar, or debate." Mere participation in a discussion gfoup, even with advance
preparation, does not qualiff as a "speech given." (AtnuaterAdvice Letter, No. A-95-
1681, Hoehn Advice Letter, No. I-92-392, (copies enclosed.).)
However, "honorarium" does not include "earned income," which is defined in
section 82030.5 as follows:
"(a) For purposes of this title, 'eamed income, means,
except as provided in subdivision (b), income from wages,
salaries, professional fees, and other amounts received or
promised to be received as compensation for personal
services rendered."
The factors that except "eamed income" from the definition of "honorarium,, are
set forth in regulation 78932:
"(a) 'Honorarium' does not include income earned for
personal services ifi


File No. A-04-069
Page No. 4
"(1) The services are provided in connection with an
individual's business or the individual's practice of or
employment in a bona fide business, hade, orprofession,
such as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real
estate, banking, or building contracting, pursuant to
California Code of Regulations, Title 2, Sections l}932J
through 18932.3; and
"(2) The services are customarilyprovided in connection
with the business, trade, orprofession."
Based on the facts that you have provided, there is nothing to suggest that your
sifuation fits within any earned income exception.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
B. Armstrong
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures
HBAjg
